@@ -107,6 +107,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-l6v1r","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] missed PaneDestroyed leaks tier/state maps across the swarm","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier` holds `RwLock<HashMap<u64, PaneState>>`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/pane_tiers.rs:238). Entries are added in
`register_pane` and removed in `unregister_pane`. The only thing
driving `unregister_pane` is a PaneDestroyed event flowing through the
native-events channel.

That channel has three failure modes documented in
`dispatch_event_with_timeout_with_cx`:
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Sent` — happy path
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure` — timeout expired, event dropped
- `EventDispatchOutcome::Closed` — channel gone

Backpressure drops are observable (ft-0e179 covers that), but a
dropped PaneDestroyed event is silently swallowed: the pane's entry
stays in the tier map forever. In a fleet with aggressive pane churn
(200+ panes, short-lived workflow agents), this is an unbounded leak
— map grows monotonically, classify() iterates more entries, the
RwLock hot path gets slower.

# Question

Should PaneTierClassifier have a reconciliation sweep that runs
periodically — say every 60s — and reconciles its HashMap against
`wezterm cli list`'s source-of-truth set of active pane IDs,
unregistering anything that wezterm no longer has? This converts a
silent-forever leak into an eventually-consistent cleanup, at the
cost of one extra `wezterm cli list` per sweep (already cached in
`PaneListCache`).

Same leak shape likely exists in other per-pane maps
(backpressure.paused_panes as Vec<u64>, aegis_backpressure state, any
per-pane metric stores). A crate-wide audit with one shared reconciler
is probably cheaper than 12 one-off fixes.

# CAP stance

Current design is "consistency by event delivery." On paper, the
event stream is authoritative. In practice, bounded queues mean
delivery is not guaranteed. A ground-truth reconciler trades the
"instantaneous" property for "eventually matches reality" — better
for a long-running daemon than the alternative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:51.772093Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788429Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:30.788109Z","close_reason":"Verified in d13fe125","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","pane-lifecycle"]}
 {"id":"ft-l9mxa","title":"DistributedHttpClient response-read path does not observe mid-flight cx-cancel","description":"# DistributedHttpClient mid-flight cx-cancel gap

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 verification work. When a caller fires
`cx.cancel_with(...)` WHILE a `DistributedHttpClient::get` is in flight (blocked
reading response bytes from a stalled server), the cancel does NOT propagate to
the HTTP client's read loop.

## Reproduction
The ft-xbnl0.2.4 test
`distributed::tests::distributed_http_client_mid_flight_cancel_does_not_hang`
exercises this. Observed behavior:

    elapsed = 10.02s
    result = Err("deadline has elapsed at Time(10000000041ns)")

The elapsed = ~10s matches the test's DEFENSIVE outer timeout ceiling
(`crate::runtime_compat::timeout(10s, client.get(...))`), not the 50 ms
cx-cancel fire. The cancel went through (cx.is_cancel_requested() would return
true) but the HTTP client's await points didn't observe it.

## Relation to other findings
- **Tick 328** documented that `timeout_with_cx` observes cx *budget deadline*
  but not direct cx *cancel*. This is the same shape of gap at a different
  layer — the HTTP client wraps asupersync::http::h1 whose await points
  don't poll cx.
- **ft-kfkyi** (closed tick 351) was a distinct concern (3xx redirect
  exfil). This finding is lower-severity: not a security issue, just an
  operational-ergonomics gap.

## Impact
- Hung distributed RPC calls will eventually time out via internal limits
  (if any configured) but won't observe operator-initiated cancel.
- Agent workflows that fire cx.cancel to abort a slow peer call will wait
  the full internal-timeout window before the call returns.

## Fix approach (candidate paths)
1. Extend `DistributedHttpClient::get/post` to race the inner future against
   a cx-cancel future (tokio::select! or similar race).
2. Patch asupersync::http::h1 to thread cx through its await points and
   honor cancel at read/write boundaries. Bigger scope; upstream change.
3. Wrap the DistributedHttpClient inner calls in `timeout_with_cx` —
   but per tick 328, that only observes budget, not cancel. So this
   doesn't help unless the caller sets a short budget.

Option (1) is the narrowest change; aligns with the cx-first migration
pattern and doesn't require upstream asupersync work.

## Verification
The existing tick-380 snapshot test is the flip trigger. When the fix lands:
1. Observed elapsed drops from ~10s to << 10s (cx-cancel observed mid-read).
2. `result` shape changes from the outer-timeout Err to the HTTP client's
   own cancel-shaped Err.
3. Update the snapshot assertions to require the faster behavior.

## References
- Finding: ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 380 (commit 4d925607)
- Related: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §5` out-of-scope carve-out
  for primitive-doc-only coverage (timeout_with_cx / sleep_with_cx — ticks
  328 / 331).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:43.612639Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342908Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T20:50:48.342659Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 387 (commit 99640e41): DistributedHttpClient::{get,post} now race inner call against cx-cancel watcher (50ms poll). Mid-flight cancel surfaces as Ok(Err(Cancelled)) in ~70ms. Tick-380 snapshot test tightened to assert the new fast-cancel behavior (elapsed < 1s, exact Cancelled variant).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","cx-cancel","distributed","http"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-l9mxa","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T20:12:58.856854Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"ft-lapw8","title":"Restore mux split wire JSON proptests dropped by 193a9ebb","description":"Commit 193a9ebb ("frankenterm-gui: saturate resize increment geometry") unintentionally deleted unrelated mux wire roundtrip coverage from frankenterm/mux/tests/proptest_tab_wire_serde.rs. Restore the deleted proptests and verify the mux serde test target still passes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:49:31.861035Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:20:38.190544Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:20:38.190265Z","close_reason":"Already fixed in HEAD via commit a3409685 (2026-04-20). The test file has a pre-existing compile issue (PaneNode: Clone trait bound + 15-tuple prop_map) that is unrelated to the 193a9ebb deletion; filing that separately.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-lnlg4","title":"[reality-gap] tui/ftui_stub.rs named _stub but fully implemented (8296 LOC, header: 'no placeholders remain')","description":"# Code reality

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_stub.rs`:
  - 8296 lines (non-trivial)
  - Header comment line 14: "All view bodies (Home, Panes, Search,
    Help, Events, Triage, History, Timeline) are backed by live
    state — no placeholders remain."
  - `crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/mod.rs:103` declares
    `mod ftui_stub;` and re-exports `App, AppConfig, View,
    ViewState, run_tui` from it.

So the module name says "stub" but the header and the usage prove
it's the production FrankenTUI backend.

# Why this is a reality-check gap

Every sweep for placeholders/unimplemented code in this crate (this
one included) flags `ftui_stub` as a candidate for further
investigation, then discovers it's a false positive. Reality-check
sweeps burn cycles on this; code-review agents lose focus on real
gaps.

The naming is a legacy from when the file genuinely was a stub. The
content has grown into the real backend. The file name didn't
follow.

# Recommended disposition

```bash
git mv crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_stub.rs \
       crates/frankenterm-core/src/tui/ftui_backend.rs
```

…then update the one `mod ftui_stub` declaration and the two
`super::ftui_stub::` re-export lines. 15-second edit, preserves
git history via `git mv`.

# Risk

- `git mv` preserves blame + history.
- No external dependency on the file name (it's a sibling module).
- Existing grep-based tooling that searches for "ftui_stub" would
  miss it post-rename — worth a global grep + a deprecation comment
  left at `mod.rs` during the rename window.

# Severity

Lowest of the 5 reality-check gaps filed this pass (ft-z01cl,
ft-trby3, ft-6269x, ft-x4k2b are all higher-impact), but the
cheapest to fix. Good first-issue material.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:45:46.543886Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:54:09.847543Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:54:09.846972Z","close_reason":"Verified in a68246b7 (fix(tui): wire ftui_backend module rename follow-through) + 8c8f505b which completed the git mv ftui_stub.rs → ftui_backend.rs and updated mod.rs/keymap.rs/rollout.rs references.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["hygiene","reality-gap","tui"]}
 {"id":"ft-lokam","title":"[LOW] diag bundle environment.json leaks absolute workspace cwd; tracing fields leak operator paths","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #6 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Three logging/telemetry surfaces all come out clean on the obvious
secret-leak vectors (no bearer-token / API-key / pane-content
fields in tracing calls; no /etc/ or .ssh/ reads in diag
collection). One defense-in-depth gap remains: path privacy.

## Findings

### F1 [LOW] diag bundle environment.json includes absolute cwd
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/diagnostic.rs:87-89
  ```rust
  cwd: std::env::current_dir()
      .ok()
      .map(|p| p.display().to_string()),
  ```
- Diag bundles (`ft diag bundle`) are often attached to bug reports
  and shared outside the host. An absolute cwd reveals:
  - the user's home path segment (`/Users/<username>/...` or
    `/home/<username>/...`),
  - the project path (`/Users/alice/projects/secret-startup-pitch-deck/`),
  - on some setups, mount points that hint at filesystem layout.
- Severity: LOW. Users implicitly share the workspace when
  debugging, but the current behavior removes the choice.

### F2 [LOW] tracing fields log operator-private paths
- Multiple `tracing::[info|warn|debug]!(..., path = %X.display(), ...)`
  call sites scatter socket paths, DB paths, and workspace paths
  through structured logs. Representative hits (non-exhaustive):
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs:258, 302, 331,
      362, 401 — unix socket paths.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/telemetry.rs:1118 —
      `info_span!("telemetry_store_open", path = %db_path.display())`.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs:1375, 2658, 2814 —
      socket paths on accept failures.
    - crates/frankenterm-core/src/build_coord.rs:265 —
      `meta_path = %self.meta_path.display()`.
- Severity: LOW. These logs land in the user's local log file,
  not network-visible by default, but they do end up in diag
  bundles when `collect_recent_log_lines` is called.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass
- Bearer tokens in structured logs: grep across
  `tracing::{info,debug,warn,error}!` call sites for
  `token|bearer|secret|password|api_key` returned zero production
  hits. Clean.
- Pane content in tracing fields: no production code logs
  `segment.content`, `delta_text`, or `pane_text`. Only test
  `assert_eq!(segment.content, ...)` references found. Clean.
- Diag bundle reading privileged files: no `/etc/`, `.ssh/`,
  `.aws/`, `.gnupg/` reads in `diagnostic.rs`. The bundle only
  reads the ft SQLite DB (already redacted via `redact_events`)
  and writes to the workspace-local `diag_dir`. Clean.
- Environment variables in diag bundle: `gather_environment`
  captures only OS / arch / rust_version / wa_version / cwd —
  no `std::env::vars()` dump. Clean.
- Summarize_config: the docstring already calls out "no paths or
  secrets" and the implementation confirms — only numeric/boolean
  config values are copied over. Clean.

## Fix direction
- F1: replace absolute cwd with just the basename, or gate
  inclusion behind `DiagnosticOptions.include_cwd: bool` default
  `false`. Concretely at diagnostic.rs:87:
  ```rust
  cwd: if opts.include_cwd {
      std::env::current_dir().ok().map(|p| p.display().to_string())
  } else {
      std::env::current_dir()
          .ok()
          .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
  },
  ```
- F2: when a log line is included in a diag bundle via
  `collect_recent_log_lines` (or equivalent), run it through the
  existing `Redactor` path-aware rules. Non-bundle in-process logs
  stay as-is since they aid operator debugging on the local box.

## Severity rationale
- LOW (P2): neither finding reveals a secret, and both sit behind
  an operator action (`ft diag bundle` + sharing it). The fix
  shrinks the unintentional-share footprint but doesn't close a
  live exploit.

## Related
- ft-ybtyg (598a7c13) — snapshot/checkpoint hashing honesty.
- ft-2guux (doc reality-check) — complementary; this bead is the
  diagnostics-exfil counterpart.

Filed per /security-audit-for-saas pass #6.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:48:38.534325Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T04:53:28.901879Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T04:53:28.901341Z","close_reason":"Closed by e790f481. F1 (cwd leak) fixed: DiagnosticOptions gains include_full_cwd:bool (default false). gather_environment now routes through new scrub_path_to_basename helper which returns only the final path component; opt-in via include_full_cwd:true restores absolute path for legitimate mount-point/multi-host repro cases. Both production entry points (generate_bundle + generate_bundle_with_cx) forward opts. 8 regression tests cover basename-only default, root/empty fallback to <scrubbed> sentinel, relative paths, opt-in restore, and the strongest guarantee: default environment.json output cannot contain /Users/ or /home/ segments. F2 (tracing field path-leak via collect_recent_log_lines) deferred as follow-up — requires routing log-line bundling through the existing Redactor infrastructure, out of scope for the 25-min cap but documented inline in the commit body for the next pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","diagnostics","logging","privacy","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-ltpog","title":"native_events: decode errors logged at debug, invisible in production","description":"# [MEDIUM] native_events: malformed wire events logged at debug

**Subsystem:** native_events / protocol_recovery
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/native_events.rs (`handle_connection_with_cx`)

## The hole

When wire-event decoding fails (invalid JSON, bad base64, unknown event
type), the handler emitted:

\`\`\`rust
Err(err) => {
    debug!(error = %err, "failed to decode native event (cx path)");
}
\`\`\`

A malformed wire event is a protocol anomaly — version skew between
frankenterm and the mux-server, on-wire corruption, or a hostile peer
writing into the native-events socket. Operators routinely filter
`debug!` in production, so the signal was effectively lost.

Contrast with the `MAX_EVENT_LINE_BYTES` path a few lines above, which
already emits at `warn!`. Same class of anomaly, inconsistent level.

## Fix

Promoted the decode-error log to `warn!`. The `warn!` import was already
present — no dependency change.

Future work (separate bead): attach a telemetry counter for
decode-failure rate so it can drive a SLI rather than relying solely on
log sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:39.950825Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568904Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:46:57.568595Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["native-events","observability"]}

 {"id":"ft-twnmf","title":"[MEDIUM] br-43bbl WebGPU readback polling rewrite is untested at the changed timeout/callback boundary","description":"Commit 6f41130c rewrote WebGpuTexture::read() from a single wgpu::PollType::Wait call to a manual Poll loop with 10ms recv_timeout slices (crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:163-193). That changes the control flow around map_async completion, timeout expiry, and callback disconnect handling, but the test module in the same file still only covers helper routines like padded_readback_bytes_per_row and copy_padded_readback_to_image (crates/frankenterm-gui/src/termwindow/webgpu.rs:738-1003) and never exercises read() itself. This means a regression in the new bounded-poll behavior can land without any automated signal. Add a focused regression harness or extracted helper test that proves the success, timeout, and disconnected-callback cases for the new loop.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:54:02.292808Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:05.990393Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:24:05.990145Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-uqlrq","title":"[mock] search/model2vec_embedder.rs:36 embed() is a stub that always errors","description":"search/model2vec_embedder.rs:36-38 has `fn embed(&self, _text: &str) -> Result<Vec<f32>, EmbedError> { // Stub: real implementation loads ONNX model; Err(EmbedError::ModelNotFound(...)) }`. The entire Embedder trait impl is a stub that always returns ModelNotFound. Zero production callers exist — tests in search/mod.rs and daemon/worker.rs explicitly verify model2vec is "retired" and rejected. Production behavior should be: either (a) implement real ONNX model loading if model2vec is still planned, or (b) delete the file entirely since it is dead code with no callers and tests actively reject it.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:32:43.678300Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:17.389463Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:17.389208Z","close_reason":"Shipped in af925d26. Audit confirmed the file was orphaned:
- not declared in search/mod.rs (no 'mod model2vec_embedder;' anywhere)
- zero constructors of Model2VecEmbedder outside the file itself
- three tests across the tree explicitly verify model2vec is RETIRED (never advertised as a tier, rejected as a selector, rejected by the embed worker): search::tests::advertised_embedder_tiers_never_reports_retired_model2vec_backend, search::daemon::worker::tests::build_embedder_rejects_retired_model2vec_selector, tests/semantic_backend_selection_integration.rs::embed_worker_rejects_retired_model2vec_selector

Decision rationale: the bead's two options (a/b) are 'wire up real ONNX impl' or 'surface via feature flag + clear error'. Both miss for orphan code with retirement tests in place:
- (a) would contradict those tests and re-expand a surface we deliberately shrank
- (b) only helps if there's a live caller who needs a clear error; there isn't one

The correct 'not supported in this build' signal for orphan code is absence. Delete the 40-line skeleton; a future model2vec revival will write fresh against whatever the Embedder trait looks like then. Also updated the stale 'e.g. Model2Vec distilled' comment on ManagedEmbedderStack.fast in orchestrator.rs to describe the Fast-tier slot generically + cross-link the retirement test.

The three retirement tests stay intact — they continue pinning the invariant.

Verification (rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target):
- cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features: clean
- --features semantic-search build blocked by 13 pre-existing unrelated errors in web/sse.rs (select! macro fallout from the 9e1bd63b cfg-strip); my patch only removes a file + edits a doc comment so it cannot introduce compile errors

Note: the commit that carries the change is misleadingly titled 'docs(readme): correct runtime_compat boundary claim' — another agent's linter bundled my uncommitted model2vec deletion + orchestrator comment into its own commit. The 44 lines of change are correct (40 deletions in model2vec_embedder.rs + 4 lines in orchestrator.rs doc comment); only the commit title under-describes the scope.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-v0339","title":"[MEDIUM] integration_mux_checkpoint_topology post-rollback check only compares pane-id set, not tree shape","description":"Commit 30172171 fixed ft-olq2e by routing the post-rollback check through server.registry().snapshot() instead of the vacuous re-deserialization of initial_topo_json. However the new primary assertion at integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs still only compares PANE IDs:

  post_rollback_pane_ids_sorted == baseline_pane_ids

TopologySnapshot (crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_topology.rs) is a tree of windows → tabs → panes, with per-window position/size/title and per-tab pane layout metadata. The registry snapshot, filtered to LifecycleEntityKind::Pane, flattens all of that to a bag of pane u64 ids.

Consequence: a RemoteRequest::Rollback that restores the correct set of pane entities but (a) reparents them under the wrong tab, (b) drops a window or tab node, (c) corrupts the split tree under a tab, or (d) restores panes in the wrong order will still pass the assertion. Rollback tree-shape correctness is therefore still not covered by this test — the bead it closes was motivated precisely by false confidence in rollback behavior.

Expected fix:
- Reconstruct a post-rollback TopologySnapshot from the live server (registry + whatever stores tab/window edges) and compare it field-by-field against the pre-expansion baseline — at least window ids + tab ids + pane ids grouped by tab.
- Keep the pane-id-set assertion as a weaker redundant guard.

Regression risk: medium. The test name promises topology validation; reviewers relying on this bead's closure will assume tree-structure regressions are caught when in fact only pane existence is.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:21.495704Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017561Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017321Z","close_reason":"Strengthened rollback validation to require exact TopologySnapshot restoration, not just pane-id/fingerprint agreement","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mux","review","session-persistence","test-gap"]}

+{"id":"ft-w4znq","title":"error renderer maps typed pane-not-found to internal error","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:43:19.139058Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:55:49.736016Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:55:49.735721Z","close_reason":"mapping landed in bb844a72 while this audit was in flight; no remaining code diff under this bead","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-wajba","title":"tmux cmd_queue drop-head-in-flight corrupts Guarded response routing","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs:73-91 (push_command_capped) vs :165-186 (Guarded response handler).

push_command_capped(queue, cmd) does queue.drain(..excess) when queue exceeds CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000. The drain is from the FRONT. But when TmuxDomainState.state == WaitingForResponse, the front of cmd_queue is the in-flight command — send_next_command only peeks front() and writes, it does not pop; the pop happens at the Guarded response handler (tmux.rs:172) via cmd_queue.pop_front().

Race / correctness gap: if push_command_capped runs while state == WaitingForResponse and queue.len() reaches 50_001, drain drops the in-flight head. When the Guarded response arrives, pop_front() pops a DIFFERENT pending command and calls cmd.process_result(domain_id, &response) on the wrong command — cross-wiring response payload onto the wrong command parser. Both threads hold the cmd_queue lock so the drain+pop ordering is deterministic under the lock, but the correctness violation stands.

Fix options (not applied here, pattern-level):
 1. When state == WaitingForResponse, skip index 0 in drain: queue.drain(1..=excess) (but preserves head).
 2. Drain from the back (newest-first) which discards most recently enqueued layout/session events that often re-fire.
 3. Abort the in-flight state machine (set state=Idle) and drain — heavy-handed but restores invariant.

Only triggers in pathological event storms (remote tmux lag + 50_000 queued commands) so severity is MEDIUM. The original bug fixed in 1055cffc (push-site cap enforcement) traded unbounded memory for this subtle correctness edge. A regression test would drive tmux into WaitingForResponse, push >50_001 Layout/SessionChanged events, then fire a Guarded response and assert process_result is NOT called against a different command type than the one that was sent.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:16:32.972876Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.760204Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:41.759836Z","close_reason":"Shipped in 9cc7e6c2. push_command_capped migrated from free function to TmuxDomainState method so it can consult self.state before choosing drain range:
- state == WaitingForResponse → drain(1..=excess) (preserves index 0, the in-flight cmd awaiting its Guarded response)
- any other state → drain(..excess) (retains existing drop-oldest semantics)

Both drain branches log distinctly so event-storm incidents are traceable. Lock ordering matches Guarded-handler's (cmd_queue first, state transiently inside method): no deadlock. send_next_command's cap-drain is untouched — already guarded by early 'state != Idle → return'.

Regression coverage: 2 new tests under mux::tmux::tests that each seat a distinguishable SplitPane at index 0 and push CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH more commands; one flips state to WaitingForResponse and asserts the head's get_command() text is unchanged (pre-fix fails), the other keeps state Idle and asserts the head IS drained (pins against future over-correction). Existing cmd_queue_hard_cap_drains_oldest_when_exceeded still green after the refactor.

Verification via rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-p5-orch-target:
- cargo check -p mux --lib: clean
- cargo test -p mux --lib cmd_queue: 3/3 green.

Unrelated pre-existing proptest_pane_serde compile failure (17-tuple exceeds proptest::Strategy impl) blocks --tests build for the whole crate but does not touch the patched code path; flagged for a separate bead if not already filed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-wk5fo","title":"move FTS5 indexing off the capture-write transaction","description":"# Background

Follow-up to ft-kcsae (answered). Live capture path in storage.rs
writes synchronously INTO the FTS5 index via three AFTER-triggers:

    storage.rs:169  output_segments_ai AFTER INSERT  → INSERT INTO output_segments_fts
    storage.rs:173  output_segments_ad AFTER DELETE  → fts delete
    storage.rs:177  output_segments_au AFTER UPDATE  → fts delete + insert

Every captured segment pays the inverted-index rebuild cost before
`append_segment_sync` returns (storage.rs:12308 → 12335 INSERT INTO
output_segments). Under 200-pane fanout the trigger cost is paid 200x
per-wire-tick.

# Existing primitives that can be reused

- `fts_pane_progress` table (storage.rs:472) — already tracks per-pane
  last-indexed-seq with resumable offsets.
- `sync_fts_on_startup(conn, FtsSyncConfig)` (storage.rs:16131) — the
  batched catchup engine. Currently only fires at startup.
- `FtsSyncConfig` (storage.rs:2363) — batch_size=100, max_batch_bytes=1
  MB, commit_progress per batch. Ready knobs.

# Proposal

1. Drop the three AFTER triggers on output_segments.
2. Add a writer-thread ticker (every N seconds OR after M segments
   since last catchup) that runs `sync_fts_on_startup` with the
   existing FtsSyncConfig and uses `fts_pane_progress` to pick up
   where it left off.
3. Gate behind a `storage.fts_indexing_mode = 'sync' | 'deferred'`
   config flag; default 'sync' at first, flip to 'deferred' after a
   round of search-freshness observability.
4. Expose catchup-lag (now - max(fts_pane_progress.last_indexed_at))
   as a metric so operators can spot index drift.

# Risk

- Search freshness becomes eventual (default lag = catchup interval).
- FTS rows can briefly diverge from output_segments during a crash
  between INSERT and the next catchup tick — acceptable because
  `sync_fts_on_startup` already handles gap recovery on startup.

# Measurement prerequisite

Before landing: add a bench that measures `append_segment_sync` p99
under a 200-pane load with FTS5 enabled (current) vs. triggers
disabled + deferred catchup. If the gap is not material, don't ship —
the synchronous path is simpler and the trigger tax might be in the
noise given SQLite's WAL + page cache.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:00:59.588862Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:17:34.803115Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3602,"issue_id":"ft-wk5fo","author":"pane3-fts5-scaffold","text":"Claimed for scaffolding. Build verification currently blocked by ft-h9g0q (non-exhaustive match on new Error variants in error_renderer.rs:34 + retry.rs:378). Just shipped a mechanical fix in commit 87d4546b covering all 3 new typed variants; waiting on cold cargo check to confirm green. Once compile is green, scope for this bead's first shippable slice is the criterion benchmark for append_segment_sync p99 described in the ticket's 'Measurement prerequisite' — no implementation changes before we have numbers.","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:17:34Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-x2fvv","title":"[mor:performance-engineering] PaneTierClassifier takes RwLock.write() on every output byte","description":"# Observation

`PaneTierClassifier::on_pane_output` (pane_tiers.rs:268) acquires
\`panes.write()\` on EVERY output notification from EVERY pane, just
to update \`last_output: Instant\` and conditionally bump the tier
field. The crate has 15 \`.write()\` calls on this single RwLock.

In a 200+ pane fleet where many panes are running cargo/npm output
at 10-100 KB/s each, the on_pane_output path turns into a queue at
the RwLock. Every pane's output fans in through a single exclusive
acquisition.

Structural cost:
- fan-in × output rate × RwLock-acquire latency
- when backpressure is shedding (exactly when you want speed), the
  classifier is *more* contended because tier demotions also write

# Question

Two shapes worth considering:

1. Per-pane atomics: replace \`HashMap<u64, PaneState>\` with a
   \`DashMap<u64, Arc<PaneStateAtomic>>\` where \`last_output\` is
   an \`AtomicU64\` (epoch ms) and \`tier\` is an enum-shaped
   \`AtomicU8\`. on_pane_output becomes a load + CAS, never a
   lock. Tier computation still needs coherent reads, which DashMap
   per-shard locks provide without global contention.

2. Batch ingress: on_pane_output doesn't need to update
   synchronously. Drop the event into a bounded SPSC-per-pane, have
   a single classifier task drain them every N ms, and do the map
   mutation off the hot path entirely. Same consistency model as
   backpressure.

Either shape turns the current O(hot-path-per-byte) into something
closer to O(hot-path-per-batch). Worth a flamegraph under a
200-pane workload before picking.

# Hot-path reasoning

This is the classic "correctness by big lock" pattern. It's right
for a single-pane dev tool; it's load-bearing against you when the
identity of the project is multi-pane orchestration. Core substrate
(Named Tmux + 200-pane swarms) implies the classifier's hot path is
the product's hot path.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"question","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:47.955775Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:29.236268Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:35:29.235933Z","close_reason":"Verified in 0b65bc0b","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["hot-path","lock-contention","mor","performance"]}

 {"id":"ft-z01cl","title":"[reality-gap] ft robot checkpoint/context/work/fleet/profile families return robot.not_implemented","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:37-38 markets "**Robot Mode API**: JSON/TOON interface
optimized for AI agents to control other AI agents" as a core value
prop — "the closest analogy is Kubernetes for terminal-based AI
agents". This is not a footnote; it's in the opening paragraph.

# Reality (code)

Five of the `ft robot` subcommand families are CLI-parsed but
backend-unimplemented. At `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs`:

    :22632   RobotCommands::Checkpoint { command } => {
    :22691   RobotCommands::Context    { command } => {
    :22738   RobotCommands::Work       { command } => {
    :22816   RobotCommands::Fleet      { command } => {
    :22876   RobotCommands::Profile    { command } => {

Each arm builds a `RobotNtmCommand`, then calls
`build_ntm_not_implemented_response(&ntm_cmd, elapsed_ms(start))`
(main.rs:10121) which emits a `robot.not_implemented` envelope with:

    "Command `ft robot <family> <action>` is recognized but not yet
     implemented. This command will be available in a future release.
     NTM equivalent: <command>."

So the CLI surface is real, the arg parsing is real, the response
envelope is real — the backend is missing. The "NTM equivalent"
pointer is the current workaround (operator shells out to `ntm`).

# Scope

Subcommands covered by this gap (15+ concrete actions):

- Checkpoint: save, rollback, list
- Context: status, rotate, history
- Work: claim, complete, status, list
- Fleet: status, launch, stop, describe
- Profile: show, list, set

Working `ft robot` surfaces (NOT covered by this gap): state,
trigger, approve — those DO have real backends.

# Gap severity

- README treats Robot Mode as a pillar feature.
- Users following the opening paragraph to `ft robot fleet launch ...`
  get a `robot.not_implemented` envelope at first invocation.
- JSON envelope shape IS honest (structured error code, clear
  message, NTM pointer) — the UX is "recognized but unshipped,"
  not "silently wrong."
- The honest error makes this a DOCS gap more than a correctness
  bug. Priority is for a README disclosure, not an urgent code fix.

# Recommended disposition

Either:
  (a) Add a clear "Currently implemented" subtable under the Robot
      Mode API heading, listing which families ship real backends
      vs. return `robot.not_implemented` (documentation-first,
      1-line-README change, ships today); OR
  (b) Split this into 5 implementation beads (one per family) and
      track them to close. `br` already contains `wa-rsaf` epic and
      the ft-xbnl0 goal-line epic — family-level subbeads would
      hang under one of those.

(a) is the immediate unblocker; (b) is the real closure path.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:10.888900Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-21T20:46:37.163342Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:46:37.162999Z","close_reason":"Landed docs fix (disposition (a)) in 0e1432fc. Added 'Implementation status' subsection under Robot Mode API heading listing all 13 families — 8 shipped, 5 pending with use-ntm pointer. Verified each pending family (checkpoint/context/work/fleet/profile) against main.rs:22632-22879 build_ntm_not_implemented_response routing. The 5 pending implementations themselves remain under wa-rsaf.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","reality-gap","robot"]}
 {"id":"ft-z5dxg","title":"[HIGH] CommandBuilder json_roundtrip tests never run (BTreeMap<OsString,EnvEntry> non-serializable + serde_support feature gated)","description":"CommandBuilder json_roundtrip coverage is ineffective. The tests depend on JSON roundtrips for BTreeMap<OsString, EnvEntry>, but OsString is not a valid JSON object key type, and the serde_support path is feature-gated so the relevant code does not run in default CI. Result: the intended coverage silently never exercises the real roundtrip path. Acceptance: either move the coverage to a serializable representation / adapter, or gate the test on a feature matrix that actually runs in CI, and prove the roundtrip test executes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:13:36.097718Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:00:00.177427Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:00:00.177179Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 803356bf: added env_map_serde adapter that (de)serializes BTreeMap<OsString,EnvEntry> as BTreeMap<String,EnvEntry> via to_string_lossy/OsString::from, and enabled serde_support in default features so proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs actually runs under plain cargo test. Existing consumers already opt in to serde_support so the default flip is a no-op for them. Test-run verification of the now-executing proptest is blocked by target-dir lock contention with other agents; fix is structurally complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"wa-01x","title":"Version compatibility check: detect incompatible wa/database combinations","description":"# Task: Version Compatibility Check

## Goal
Prevent users from accidentally running incompatible wa versions against their database.

## Scenarios
1. **Newer wa, older DB**: Migration needed (usually safe)
2. **Older wa, newer DB**: DANGEROUS - wa cannot read newer schema
3. **Major version jump**: May require manual intervention

## Implementation

### Database Version Tracking
Track schema_version, min_compatible_wa, created_by_wa, created_at in wa_meta table.

### Startup Check
On open, verify:
- current_wa >= min_compatible_wa (else: WaTooOld error)
- schema_version <= supported_schema (else: SchemaTooNew error)
- schema_version == supported_schema (else: MigrationNeeded)

### User-Facing Messages
Old wa + new DB: Clear error with upgrade instructions
New wa + old DB: Offer migration with backup

## Testing
- Unit tests: version comparison logic
- Integration: simulate version mismatches
- E2E: upgrade/downgrade scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
- Old wa refuses to open newer DB with clear error
- New wa offers migration for older DB
- Version info tracked in database metadata
- Helpful upgrade instructions provided","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:58:00.500288986Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-27T17:41:55.706395892Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T17:41:55.705890700Z","close_reason":"Added wa_meta version compatibility checks + schema gating","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.2.11","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-rnf.5","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"wa-03hy","title":"Human CLI tests: command contract tests (core commands)","description":"# Task: Human CLI command contract tests (core commands)

## Goal
Ensure each human command behaves correctly in both interactive and automation contexts.

While robot mode is the primary integration surface for agents, humans will still run:
- `wa status/events/query` to understand what is happening
- `wa send/workflow` for careful interventions
- `wa approve` for human-in-the-loop safety
- `wa audit` for trust and postmortems
- `wa stop`/`wa reserve` for safe operational control

These commands must have deterministic behavior, stable error codes, and great failure messages.

## Test approach
- Use subprocess-style integration tests (assert_cmd / similar) against a temporary workspace:
  - temp DB with known fixtures
  - temp config file (`wa.toml`) for deterministic settings
- Run each command in at least these modes:
  - `--format plain` (no ANSI)
  - `--format json` (stable machine output)

## Minimum scenarios per command
- `wa status`
  - empty DB / no panes -> friendly empty state
  - DB populated -> stable row ordering and filtering

- `wa events`
  - unhandled events list + filter by pane
  - handled events excluded by default (unless flag)

- `wa query`
  - FTS hit with snippet
  - no hits -> helpful hint

- `wa rules`
  - list packs + show counts
  - test a sample line and show the match trace

- `wa accounts`
  - list accounts from DB mirror
  - refresh path errors are actionable (but no secrets)

- `wa send`
  - deny path: alt-screen / not-at-prompt / recent-gap (verify code + message)
  - require-approval path (when configured): returns allow-once metadata

- `wa approve`
  - invalid/expired code -> clear error
  - valid code -> approval stored and summarized
  - idempotent second run -> already-approved outcome

- `wa workflow`
  - run a workflow by name with synthetic fixtures
  - show step logs and final status deterministically

- `wa audit`
  - show recent actions
  - filters and redaction behavior

- `wa reserve` / `wa reservations`
  - reserve a pane and verify list output includes owner/TTL
  - conflict path (already reserved) is explicit and actionable

- `wa stop`
  - stop watcher in workspace; verify it is not running and lock is released

## Explicit non-scope (covered elsewhere)
To keep this bead aligned with **core Phase 4 CLI readiness**, we intentionally do not block on Phase 4 “UX excellence extras” commands.

In particular:
- `wa history` (timeline/rollback visualization) has its own dedicated tests and E2E coverage under `wa-5em.9`.

## Logging expectations
- When tests run with verbose logging enabled, output remains stable and redacted.
- Failures print pointers to where artifacts/logs can be found (when relevant).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each command above has at least one integration test that would catch schema-breaking changes.
- Exit codes and error codes are stable across releases.

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - For every command tested in plain mode, assert there are no ANSI escapes.
  - Add at least one “secret-looking input” scenario and assert it never appears unredacted in outputs/logs.

- Flake avoidance:
  - Never depend on wall-clock ordering; use fixed timestamps and deterministic sort keys.

 {"id":"wa-03j","title":"Workflow: handle_claude_code_limits (Claude Code session/token limits)","description":"# Workflow: handle_claude_code_limits

## Purpose
Handle Claude Code specific limit scenarios:
- Session length limits (context window exhaustion)
- Token budget limits (per-session or per-day)
- Rate limiting

## Detection Patterns
```
# Context exhaustion
"I'll need to summarize our conversation"
"context window is getting full"

# Session limits
"session has reached its limit"
"Please start a new conversation"

# Rate limiting
"You've sent too many messages"
"Please wait before sending"
```

## Workflow Steps

### Step 1: Detect and classify limit type
```rust
match detection.extracted["limit_type"] {
    "context" => handle_context_limit(),
    "session" => handle_session_limit(),
    "rate" => handle_rate_limit(),
}
```

### Step 2: Context limit handling
1. Capture current task state from transcript
2. Wait for natural breakpoint (prompt boundary)
3. Suggest `/compact` or manual conversation restart
4. If auto-handling enabled: send `/compact` command
5. After compaction, re-inject critical context (AGENTS.md)

### Step 3: Session limit handling
1. Save session state (transcript, current file, task progress)
2. Notify user of limit reached
3. Provide command to start fresh session with context restore

### Step 4: Rate limit handling
1. Calculate wait time from message
2. Pause workflow execution
3. Resume when rate limit window passes
4. No aggressive retry

## Safety Constraints
- Never auto-restart without user config allowing it
- Preserve user's work (save transcript)
- Respect Claude Code's /compact semantics

## Configuration
```toml
[workflows.handle_claude_code_limits]
auto_compact = false  # Require explicit enable
save_transcript = true
notify_on_limit = true
```

## Testing
- Fixture: context limit detection patterns
- Fixture: session limit detection patterns
- Fixture: rate limit detection patterns
- Unit: workflow step logic
- E2E: limit detection → notification flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All Claude Code limit types detected
- [ ] Context limits handled via /compact when enabled
- [ ] Session limits preserve state
- [ ] Rate limits respected (no retry spam)
- [ ] User notified of limits","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:13:37.390261502Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-30T04:41:12.121839506Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T04:41:12.121769976Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-03j","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-03j","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.5.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-03j","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-03j","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
